items.title: Classification of strabismus
items.contentHtml:
Classification of strabismus

  • According to direction: endotropia (nasal deviation), exotropia (temporal), hypertropia/hypotropia (vertical).
  • According to latency: tropia (constant deviation) vs phoria (latent, only in occlusion).
  • According Etiology: accommodative (associated with uncorrected hyperopia), non-accommodative (mechanical, neurological).
  • According to fixation: alternating (fixed with either eye) or monocular (always the same deviated eye).

items.title: Hirschberg corneal reflex
items.contentHtml:
Hirschberg corneal reflex

  • Evaluates ocular alignment by observing the light reflection in both corneas.
  • Reflex centered in both eyes = orthotropy; decentration = strabismus.
  • Deviation of 1 mm from the pupillary center is approximately equivalent to 7° (15 prism diopters) deviation.
  • If the reflection is at the pupillary edge: ~15°; in the middle iris: ~30°; in the limbo: ~45°.
  • Useful in non-cooperative children and as a rapid screening.

items.title: Alternating occlusion test
items.contentHtml:
Alternating occlusion test

  • Detects phorias (latent deviation) when fusion is broken.
  • One eye is occluded, then quickly passed to the other; if the uncovered eye moves to fixate, there is deviation.
  • Nasal to temporal movement = exophoria; temporal to nasal = endophoria.
  • Does not measure exact magnitude; requires bar prisms to quantify (prism cover test).
  • In manifest strabismus (tropia) the deviated eye does not move when uncovering because it is already in constant deviation.

items.title: Causes of amblyopia
items.contentHtml:
Causes of amblyopia

  • Amblyopia due to strabismus: cortical suppression of the image of the deviated eye to avoid diplopia.
  • Refractive amblyopia: due to anisometropia (difference >1.5 D of spherical equivalent or >1 D of astigmatism) or bilateral ametropia high.
  • Amblyopia due to deprivation: obstruction of the visual axis (congenital cataract, severe ptosis, corneal opacity).
  • Amblyopia due to occlusion: iatrogenic due to excessive patching in a healthy eye.

items.title: Amblyopia due to strabismus
items.contentHtml:
Amblyopia due to strabismus

  • Mechanism: active cortical suppression of the image of the deviated eye to avoid diplopia.
  • It develops in constant monocular strabismus (not alternating).
  • More common in endotropia than in exotropia due to less suppression capacity.
  • Visual acuity of the amblyopic eye does not improve with refractive correction alone.
  • Treatment: occlusion of the healthy eye (patch) or pharmacological penalty (atropine 1% in healthy eye) to force use of the amblyopic eye.

items.title: Amblyopia treatment in children
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of amblyopia in children

  • First line: complete optical correction (glasses) for 4-8 weeks before starting occlusion.
  • Occlusion (patch): in healthy eye, hours/day depending on age and severity (e.g. 2-6 h/day).
  • Pharmacological penalty: atropine 1% in healthy eye (1 drop/day), alternative to patch.
  • Duration: until equal visual acuity or stable improvement is achieved; then maintenance until 8-10 years.
  • Risk of amblyopia due to iatrogenic occlusion if time is exceeded in healthy eye.

items.title: Prisms in strabismus
items.contentHtml:
Prisms in strabismus

  • Indicated for diplopia in small angle strabismus (<20 prism diopters).
  • Base of the prism oriented towards deviation: nasal base in exotropia, temporal base in endotropia.
  • Diagnostic use: prism cover test to measure deviation angle.
  • Therapeutic use: symptomatic relief in cranial nerve palsies (e.g. VI nerve) or post-surgical residual strabismus.
  • They do not correct the cause or improve fusion; They only optically compensate for the deviation.

items.title: Extraocular muscle surgery
items.contentHtml:
Extraocular muscle surgery

  • Indication: non-accommodative strabismus, stable angle, failure of conservative treatment.
  • Techniques: resection (shortens the muscle, increases its action) and recession (retroinsertion, weakens the muscle).
  • In endotropia recession of the medial rectus and/or is performed. resection of the lateral rectus.
  • In exotropia recession of the lateral rectus and/or resection of the medial rectus is performed.
  • Complications: hypo/hypocorrection, scleral perforation, adhesion syndrome, muscle loss.

items.title: Sixth cranial nerve palsy
items.contentHtml:
Palsy of the 6th cranial nerve

  • Abducens nerve: innervates the lateral rectus; failure produces endotropia due to uncounteracted action of the medial rectus.
  • Causes: microvascular (HTN, DM), trauma, intracranial hypertension, tumors (brainstem, cavernous sinus).
  • Clinic: horizontal binocular diplopia that worsens in ipsilateral lateral gaze; abduction limitation.
  • Acute management: alternating occlusion, temporal base prisms, botulinum toxin in the medial rectum for contracture.
  • If it does not resolve in 6-12 months: muscle transposition surgery (Hummelsheim).

items.title: Accommodative strabismus and its management
items.contentHtml:
Accommodative strabismus and its management

  • Associated with uncorrected hyperopia: excess accommodation generates excessive accommodative convergence → endotropia.
  • It is corrected with complete hyperopia glasses (previous cycloplegia to determine refraction).
  • CA/A ratio (accommodative convergence/accommodation) high: greater deviation in near than in distance.
  • Management: bifocal glasses (addition for near) or progressive lenses if high CA/A.
  • If deviation persists with complete optical correction, consider non-accommodative component and surgery.

items.title: Duane syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Duane syndrome

  • Congenital disorder due to abnormal innervation of the VI cranial nerve (failure to develop the nucleus abducens).
  • Type I: limitation of abduction with retraction of the globe and narrowing of the palpebral fissure in adduction.
  • Type II: limitation of adduction with retraction in abduction.
  • Type III: limitation of abduction and adduction.
  • Management: if there is endotropia in primary position or significant torticollis, surgery (medial rectus recession, transposition).

items.title: Worth method and stereopsis test
items.contentHtml:
Worth's method and stereopsis test

  • Worth's test (4 points): evaluates fusion and suppression with red-green glasses; sees 4 points = fusion; 2 red = green eye suppression; 3 green = red eye suppression.
  • Stereopsis test: measures three-dimensional binocular vision; It is performed with Titmus blades (fly, circles) or Randot.
  • Normal threshold: 40 seconds of arc (Titmus).
  • Fine stereopsis altered in strabismus and amblyopia; It is the best sensory fusion test.
  • Useful to monitor response to treatment and detect suppression.
